name: AMC Math
description: AMC 10/12 competition math, proofs, olympiad problem solving

topics.title: Vieta's Formulas
topics.explainer:
## Vieta's Formulas

### For Quadratics
If r and s are roots of ax² + bx + c = 0:
- r + s = −b/a
- r × s = c/a

### For Cubics
If r, s, t are roots of ax³ + bx² + cx + d = 0:
- r+s+t = −b/a
- rs+rt+st = c/a
- rst = −d/a

### Key Insight
You can find sums/products of roots WITHOUT finding the roots themselves!

### AMC Application
**Problem:** If r and s are roots of x²−5x+3=0, find r²+s².

**Solution:** 
- r+s = 5, rs = 3
- r²+s² = (r+s)² − 2rs = 25 − 6 = **19**

### Useful Identities
- r² + s² = (r+s)² − 2rs
- r³ + s³ = (r+s)³ − 3rs(r+s)
- 1/r + 1/s = (r+s)/(rs)

questions.question: If r,s are roots of x²−7x+10=0, what is r+s?
questions.explanation: By Vieta's: r+s = −(−7)/1 = 7.

questions.question: Roots of x²−3x+1=0. Find r²+s².
Roots of 2x²+6x−4=0. Product of roots?

topics.title: AM-GM & Cauchy-Schwarz
topics.explainer:
## Classic Inequalities

### AM-GM (Arithmetic Mean ≥ Geometric Mean)
For non-negative a, b:
(a+b)/2 ≥ √(ab)

Equality when a = b.

**General:** (a₁+a₂+...+aₙ)/n ≥ (a₁a₂...aₙ)^(1/n)

### Application
Minimize x + 1/x for x > 0:
By AM-GM: x + 1/x ≥ 2√(x·1/x) = 2
Minimum is **2** when x = 1.

### Cauchy-Schwarz
(a²+b²)(c²+d²) ≥ (ac+bd)²

More generally:
(Σaᵢ²)(Σbᵢ²) ≥ (Σaᵢbᵢ)²

### QM-AM-GM-HM Chain
Quadratic Mean ≥ AM ≥ GM ≥ Harmonic Mean
√((a²+b²)/2) ≥ (a+b)/2 ≥ √(ab) ≥ 2ab/(a+b)

questions.question: Minimum value of x + 4/x for x > 0?
AM-GM: equality holds when:
questions.explanation: AM = GM if and only if a₁ = a₂ = ... = aₙ.

topics.title: Polynomial Tricks
topics.explainer:
## Polynomial Techniques

### Remainder Theorem
The remainder when P(x) is divided by (x−a) is P(a).

### Factor Theorem
(x−a) is a factor of P(x) iff P(a) = 0.

### Rational Root Theorem
If p/q is a rational root of aₙxⁿ+...+a₀, then:
- p divides a₀
- q divides aₙ

### Sum of Roots = −b/a (Vieta's)

### Newton's Sums
Relate power sums (r₁ᵏ+r₂ᵏ+...) to elementary symmetric polynomials.

### Common Factorizations
- a³+b³ = (a+b)(a²−ab+b²)
- a³−b³ = (a−b)(a²+ab+b²)
- a³+b³+c³−3abc = (a+b+c)(a²+b²+c²−ab−bc−ca)
- If a+b+c = 0, then a³+b³+c³ = 3abc

questions.question: Remainder when x⁴+x²+1 is divided by x−1?
questions.explanation: P(1) = 1+1+1 = 3 by the Remainder Theorem.

questions.question: If a+b+c = 0, then a³+b³+c³ = ?
questions.explanation: When a+b+c=0: a³+b³+c³ = 3abc. Classic competition identity.

topics.title: Triangle Centers & Properties
topics.explainer:
## Triangle Centers

### Centroid (G)
Intersection of medians. Divides each median in 2:1 ratio.
G = ((x₁+x₂+x₃)/3, (y₁+y₂+y₃)/3)

### Circumcenter (O)
Center of circumscribed circle. Equidistant from all vertices.
Perpendicular bisectors meet here.

### Incenter (I)
Center of inscribed circle. Equidistant from all sides.
Angle bisectors meet here. r = Area/s (s = semi-perimeter)

### Orthocenter (H)
Intersection of altitudes.

### Euler Line
H, G, O are collinear! G divides HO in ratio 2:1 (HG:GO = 2:1).

### Key Formulas
- Area = rs (r = inradius, s = semi-perimeter)
- Area = abc/(4R) (R = circumradius)
- Stewart's Theorem: a²n + b²m − c·m·n = c·d² (cevian length)
topics.visual:
Triangle Centers:
  ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │         A                  │
  │        /\                  │
  │       /  \                 │
  │      / G  \  G = centroid  │
  │     / ·    \  (medians)   │
  │    /   O    \ O = circumctr│
  │   /    ·     \ (⊥ bisectors)│
  │  /      I     \ I = incenter│
  │ B───────────────C (∠ bisectors)│
  │                            │
  │ Euler Line: H, G, O       │
  │ (collinear!)               │
  └────────────────────────────┘

questions.question: The centroid divides each median in the ratio:
questions.explanation: Centroid is 2/3 of the way from vertex to opposite midpoint (ratio 2:1).

questions.question: The incircle radius r equals:
questions.explanation: Area = r × s, so r = Area/s where s = (a+b+c)/2.

questions.question: Which three triangle centers lie on the Euler line?
questions.explanation: H (orthocenter), G (centroid), O (circumcenter) are always collinear on the Euler line.

topics.title: Circle Theorems
topics.explainer:
## Circle Theorems

### Inscribed Angle Theorem
Inscribed angle = ½ central angle (same arc).

### Thales' Theorem
Angle inscribed in a semicircle = 90°.

### Power of a Point
For point P and circle:
- External: PA × PB = PC × PD (two secants)
- Internal: PA × PB = PC × PD (two chords)
- Tangent: PT² = PA × PB

### Cyclic Quadrilateral
Opposite angles sum to 180° (supplementary).

### Tangent Properties
- Tangent ⊥ radius at point of tangency
- Two tangents from external point are equal length

### Ptolemy's Theorem
For cyclic quadrilateral ABCD:
AC × BD = AB × CD + AD × BC
(Diagonal product = sum of opposite side products)

questions.question: An angle inscribed in a semicircle measures:
questions.explanation: Thales' theorem: inscribed angle in semicircle = ½ × 180° = 90°.

questions.question: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles:
questions.explanation: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary (sum = 180°).

questions.question: From external point P, tangent to circle has length 8, secant passes through at distances 4 and x. Find x.
questions.explanation: PT² = PA×PB → 64 = 4×x → x = 16.

topics.title: Area Methods & Ratios
topics.explainer:
## Area Methods

### Coordinate Area (Shoelace)
A = ½|Σ(xᵢyᵢ₊₁ − xᵢ₊₁yᵢ)|

### Area Ratios
If triangles share a vertex, their area ratio = ratio of their bases.
If they share a base, ratio = ratio of heights.

### Mass Point Geometry
Assign masses to vertices such that the triangle balances at the desired point. Useful for finding ratios of segments.

### Pick's Theorem
For lattice polygons:
A = I + B/2 − 1
- I = interior lattice points
- B = boundary lattice points

### Cross Ratio / Area Ratio Trick
In triangle ABC, if D divides BC in ratio m:n, then:
Area(ABD)/Area(ADC) = m/n

questions.question: Lattice polygon with 5 interior points and 8 boundary points. Area?
Triangle ABC: D on BC with BD:DC = 2:3. Area(ABD):Area(ABC) = ?
questions.explanation: Same height from A. Base ratio BD:BC = 2:5 → area ratio = 2:5.

number-theory-advanced.name: Number Theory
number-theory-advanced.description: Congruences, Fermat's theorem, Chinese Remainder Theorem

topics.title: Fermat's Little Theorem & Euler's
topics.explainer:
## Fermat's Little Theorem

### Statement
If p is prime and gcd(a,p)=1:
**a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p)**

Equivalently: a^p ≡ a (mod p) for any a.

### Example
2^100 mod 13:
- p=13, so 2^12 ≡ 1 (mod 13)
- 100 = 12×8 + 4
- 2^100 = (2^12)^8 × 2^4 ≡ 1^8 × 16 ≡ 3 (mod 13)

### Euler's Theorem (generalization)
If gcd(a,n)=1:
**a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n)**

where φ(n) = Euler's totient = count of integers 1..n coprime to n.

### Computing φ(n)
- φ(p) = p−1 (prime)
- φ(p^k) = p^k − p^(k−1)
- φ(mn) = φ(m)φ(n) if gcd(m,n)=1
- φ(n) = n × ∏(1 − 1/p) for each prime p|n

questions.question: By Fermat's Little Theorem, 3^6 mod 7 = ?

topics.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
topics.explainer:
## Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)

### Statement
If n₁,n₂,...,nₖ are pairwise coprime, the system:
x ≡ a₁ (mod n₁)
x ≡ a₂ (mod n₂)
...
has a unique solution modulo N = n₁n₂...nₖ.

### Example
Find x where:
- x ≡ 2 (mod 3)
- x ≡ 3 (mod 5)
- x ≡ 2 (mod 7)

N = 3×5×7 = 105
Test: x=23. 23 mod 3=2 ✓, 23 mod 5=3 ✓, 23 mod 7=2 ✓
Solution: x ≡ 23 (mod 105)

### AMC Application
Often used when asked: 'What is the smallest positive integer that gives remainder r₁ when divided by m₁ and remainder r₂ when divided by m₂?'

questions.question: Smallest positive x where x≡1(mod 3) and x≡2(mod 5)?
CRT requires the moduli to be:
questions.explanation: CRT guarantees a unique solution when all moduli are pairwise coprime (gcd of any two = 1).

topics.title: Diophantine Equations
topics.explainer:
## Diophantine Equations

Integer-only solutions to equations.

### Linear Diophantine: ax + by = c
Solution exists iff gcd(a,b) | c.

**Extended Euclidean Algorithm** finds x₀, y₀ such that ax₀+by₀ = gcd(a,b).

General solution: x = x₀ + (b/d)t, y = y₀ − (a/d)t where d = gcd(a,b).

### Pythagorean Triples
All primitive triples (a,b,c) with a²+b²=c²:
- a = m²−n², b = 2mn, c = m²+n²
- where m>n>0, gcd(m,n)=1, m−n is odd

### Pell's Equation
x² − Dy² = 1 (D not a perfect square)
Infinite solutions from continued fraction of √D.

### Sum of Two Squares
n = a²+b² iff every prime p≡3(mod 4) divides n an even number of times.

questions.question: Does 6x + 9y = 10 have integer solutions?
options: No, because gcd(6,9)=3 does not divide 10
questions.explanation: gcd(6,9)=3. Since 3 does not divide 10, no integer solutions exist.

questions.question: Using m=3, n=1 in the Pythagorean triple generator gives:
questions.explanation: a=9-1=8, b=2×3×1=6, c=9+1=10 → (6,8,10). Note: this is 2×(3,4,5), not primitive since gcd(3,1)=1 but 3-1=2 is even.

counting-probability.name: Counting & Probability
counting-probability.description: Advanced combinatorics, generating functions, expected value

topics.title: Stars and Bars & Generating Functions
topics.explainer:
## Stars and Bars

### Problem Type
Distribute n identical objects into k distinct bins.

### Formula
C(n+k−1, k−1) = C(n+k−1, n)

### With Constraints
Each bin gets ≥ 1: substitute xᵢ = yᵢ+1, solve with n−k objects.
Each bin gets ≤ M: use inclusion-exclusion.

### Example
Distribute 10 identical candies among 3 children:
C(10+3−1, 3−1) = C(12,2) = **66** ways

Each child gets ≥ 2 candies:
Give 2 to each first → 4 remaining → C(4+2,2) = C(6,2) = **15** ways

### Generating Functions
Encode counting problems as polynomial coefficients:
- Coefficient of xⁿ in (1+x+x²+...)^k = C(n+k-1, k-1)
- Each factor represents choices for one variable

questions.question: Number of ways to put 8 identical balls in 4 distinct boxes?
Distribute 7 cookies among 3 kids, each gets ≥ 1. How many ways?
questions.explanation: Give 1 each first → 4 remaining. C(4+2,2) = C(6,2) = 15.

topics.title: Expected Value & Linearity
topics.explainer:
## Expected Value

### Definition
E[X] = Σ xᵢ × P(xᵢ)

### Linearity of Expectation
**E[X+Y] = E[X] + E[Y]** (ALWAYS, even if dependent!)

### Powerful Applications

**Example 1:** Expected number of fixed points in a random permutation of n elements?
Let Xᵢ = 1 if element i is in position i.
E[Xᵢ] = 1/n.
E[X] = E[X₁]+...+E[Xₙ] = n × (1/n) = **1**

**Example 2:** Expected number of coin flips to get heads?
Geometric distribution: E[X] = 1/p = 1/(1/2) = **2**

### Indicator Random Variables
Define Xᵢ = 1 if event i occurs, 0 otherwise.
E[Xᵢ] = P(event i). Sum them up using linearity!

### Geometric Distribution
Trials until first success: E[X] = 1/p
topics.visual:
Linearity of Expectation:
  ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │  Even when X and Y are     │
  │  dependent:                │
  │                            │
  │  E[X+Y] = E[X] + E[Y]     │
  │                            │
  │  Fixed points in perm:     │
  │  E = Σ P(i stays) = n×1/n │
  │    = 1 (regardless of n!)  │
  │                            │
  │  Incredibly powerful for   │
  │  competition problems!     │
  └────────────────────────────┘

questions.question: Expected number of heads in 10 fair coin flips?
questions.explanation: E = np = 10 × 0.5 = 5. By linearity: each flip has E[Xᵢ]=0.5, sum = 5.

questions.question: Linearity of expectation works:
questions.explanation: E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] holds unconditionally — the most powerful tool in probability!

questions.question: Expected flips until first heads (fair coin)?
questions.explanation: Geometric distribution: E = 1/p = 1/(1/2) = 2.

topics.title: Competition Problem Solving Toolkit
topics.explainer:
## Competition Strategies

### 1. Try Small Cases
Can't solve for n? Try n=1,2,3 and look for patterns.

### 2. Work Backwards
Know the answer format? Work from it back to the given info.

### 3. Symmetry
Look for symmetry to simplify. If f(x)+f(1-x) = constant, use it!

### 4. Extreme Cases
Test boundary values. What happens at 0, 1, ∞?

### 5. Complementary Counting
Count what you DON'T want, subtract from total.
P(at least one) = 1 − P(none)

### 6. Invariants
Find a quantity that doesn't change. Use it to prove impossibility.

### 7. Pigeonhole Principle
n+1 objects in n boxes → some box has ≥ 2.

### 8. Parity
Odd/even arguments. Sum parity, count parity.

### Time Management (AMC)
- Easy (1-10): 2 min each
- Medium (11-20): 3-4 min each
- Hard (21-25): 5+ min each
- Skip and return to hard problems
topics.visual:
AMC Strategy Flowchart:
  ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │  Read problem carefully    │
  │         ↓                  │
  │  Try small cases?          │
  │    Yes → find pattern      │
  │    No ↓                    │
  │  Recognize known formula?  │
  │    Yes → apply directly    │
  │    No ↓                    │
  │  Complementary counting?   │
  │    Maybe → try it          │
  │    No ↓                    │
  │  Work backwards from       │
  │  answer choices!           │
  └────────────────────────────┘

questions.question: When stuck on a combinatorics problem, the best first step is often:
options: Try small cases and look for a pattern
questions.explanation: Small cases reveal patterns: try n=1,2,3 to build intuition before tackling the general case.

questions.question: 'How many integers from 1 to 100 are NOT divisible by 2 or 3?' Best approach?
questions.explanation: Count those divisible by 2 or 3 using inclusion-exclusion, subtract from 100.

topics.title: Proof Techniques
topics.explainer:
## Proof Techniques

### Direct Proof
Assume hypothesis, derive conclusion through logical steps.

### Proof by Contradiction
Assume the opposite of what you want to prove, derive a contradiction.
- Classic: √2 is irrational
- Assume √2 = p/q in lowest terms → 2q²=p² → p is even → p=2k → q is even → contradiction!

### Mathematical Induction
1. **Base case:** Prove for n=1 (or n=0)
2. **Inductive step:** Assume true for n=k, prove for n=k+1

### Strong Induction
Assume true for ALL values ≤ k, prove for k+1.

### Proof by Construction
Prove existence by explicitly constructing an example.

### Proof by Exhaustion
Check all possible cases (only when finite and manageable).

### The Well-Ordering Principle
Every non-empty set of positive integers has a smallest element.
Equivalent to induction!
topics.visual:
Mathematical Induction:
  ┌────────────────────────────┐
  │  1. Base: P(1) is true ✓  │
  │                            │
  │  2. Inductive step:        │
  │     Assume P(k) true       │
  │     Prove P(k+1) true     │
  │                            │
  │  Like dominoes:            │
  │  ┌┐┌┐┌┐┌┐┌┐┌┐┌┐           │
  │  ╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱           │
  │  Push first → all fall!    │
  └────────────────────────────┘

questions.question: To prove √2 is irrational, you would use:
questions.explanation: Assume √2 = p/q in lowest terms, derive that both p and q are even → contradiction.

questions.question: In mathematical induction, the two required steps are:
questions.explanation: Induction: (1) prove base case, (2) prove if P(k) then P(k+1).

questions.question: Strong induction differs from regular induction in that:
options: You assume P(1)...P(k) all true, not just P(k)
You need multiple base cases always
It only works for even numbers
questions.explanation: Strong induction assumes truth for all values ≤ k, giving more to work with for proving P(k+1).
